data.title: Millrose Properties: Book Value Remains A Likely Ceiling
data.description:
Shares of Millrose Properties (NYSE:MRP) have been an excellent performer since spinning out of Lennar (LEN) earlier this year, with the stock up 34% over just the past six months. The company continues to benefit from demand by homebuilders to keep their balance sheets capital light and option land through Millrose rather than own it outright themselves. I last covered shares in June, when I rated the stock a “hold” as I felt upside was becoming more limited. In hindsight, this was premature, as the stock has gained a further 16%, exceeding the market’s 10% rally. With updated financials and such strong performance, now is a good time to revisit Millrose.

In the company’s third quarter, Millrose Properties generated $0.74 of funds from operations (“FFO”), which beat estimates by $0.05, on revenue of $179 million. This is an 8.3% annualized yield on equity. The company is, quite frankly, ramping up activity more quickly than I hoped, which speaks to builders wanting to move assets off balance sheet to a land bank like MRP and simply hold an option on their land. This reduces the capital intensity of the builders’ balance sheet. Management now expects to sign $2.2 billion of deals this year, outside of its Lennar agreement, up $200 million from its prior guidance.

Concentration risk is manageable

Millrose now has nearly 139,000 homesites across 876 communities. While it operates in 30 states, this overstates the amount of diversification that the business truly has. The top three states, CA, TX, and FL, account for half of its future takedown proceeds. The TX and FL markets have been particularly weak, and so I view this geographic concentration as a modest negative. Ultimately, though, these markets are weaker because these are the markets where homebuilders have been doing the most construction, creating supply problems. Most of the industry has disproportionate Sun Belt exposure as a result.

In theory if the market were to continue to weaken, there would be a risk that builders ultimately terminate the option rather than take delivery of their lots. Ultimately, I view this risk as small. MRP’s contracts are favorable, especially its master agreement with Lennar, so that different communities are pooled. LEN cannot decline delivery in a community without also giving up access to another community. As such, even if one area is underperforming, it is unlikely to walk away and lose access to a better-performing lot. Plus, the cost of the lot is relatively low, so that we would need to see home prices fall 25+% to merit walking away. Prices in some markets are down ~5%, but there are no declines like this.

While the housing market faces some pressures, I also view a decline like this as extremely unlikely. Total inventories have risen from the lows, but they still remain fairly low. We also have not seen the excessive mortgage lending that we saw in the mid-2000s. Finally, mortgage rates have also started coming down a bit, which should help reduce pressure on homebuying, all else equal. My central case is for home prices to decline 0-3% over the next year, but I view a “crash” as unlikely, given these conditions.

Non-Lennar deals continue to ramp

During the quarter, it funded $858 million under its Lennar agreement, and it funded $770 million in third party deals at 11.1%. Its Lennar program earns a lower yield of 8.5%, as Lennar receives a benefit for being its prior owner and providing so much volume to MRP, and this rate of return is fixed. Third-party deals will be negotiated on a one-by-one basis, and I view yields north of 11% as attractive. The Lennar contract has a ~5 year life, while its acquisitions with third-party builders this quarter have a ~4 year average life.

During the quarter, it received net proceeds of $852, nearly 90% of which came from Lennar. Essentially, it simply redeployed what it received from LEN into new properties. The remaining deals were done via additional debt issuance as MRP ramps up its balance sheet to its desired leverage. As a reminder, Millrose pays a 1.25% management fee on deployed assets.

It has fully paid down its term loan by doing two successful bond offerings, with $2 billion in debt now outstanding on these notes. With this issuance, we continue to see the company making progress toward reaching its run-rate size. During the third quarter, it added $1 billion of debt and assets, and Millrose now has $9 billion of total assets. Its debt to capitalization stands at a conservative 25%.

The company expects to maintain a maximum debt-to-capital ratio of 33%. This gives it another ~$1-1.6 billion of potential debt capacity. Essentially, I see the strong demand this year as pulling forward the time at which MRP reaches its complete run-rate, but I do not really see it changing the actual earnings power once at run rate.

As of quarter end, $6.3 billion, or just under 80% of its portfolio, is with Lennar, and other agreements comprise 23% of its portfolio. The portfolio has a blended yield of 9.1%, providing about $188 million of quarterly revenue. This is $9 million above Q3 levels, reflecting a full-quarter impact of deals signed during the quarter. As it continues ramping up third party deals, its average yield should continue to tick up. Assuming remaining deals are done north of 11%, the ultimate book yield should be in the 9.5-10% zone.

Separately, in October, Lennar offered an exchange of its remaining 20% stake in MRP whereby it would issue Millrose equity and receive LEN shares. This offer ends on November 7th, and it should eliminate one last overhang for the stock. The potential of Lennar to actively sell its shares has been a negative potential technical, but this exchange to willing participants who want to own MRP should mitigate that pressure. I view this resolution as an incremental positive for investors.

Book value is the likely ceiling for shares

Now, looking forward, the company plans to distribute 100% of earnings to shareholders over time. Because it is not going to retain any capital, I struggle to see earnings growing once it has fully ramped up its business, unless its stock rallied to a point where it could issue equity to finance additional deals. However, with shares still trading at a discount to book value, I do not see Millrose issuing equity anytime soon.

Management expects run-rate FFO to be $0.74-$0.76 at year-end, and shares offer an 8.8% dividend yield. Book value ended the quarter at $35.29. Management believes the stock should trade above book value. Back in June, I saw fair value as about 0.85x book value, given an 8.5% ROE and a required return for investors of 10%. I stand by my view that MRP should trade below book value.

With a 9.5% yield, less its management fee, MRP should earn about 8.25%, assuming no homebuilder ever walks away from its properties. Assuming a long-term debt-to-capital ratio of ~30% (as you cannot always run at the maximum level) and a ~6% long-term cost of debt, this business can generate a ~9% ROE. Assuming investors have a 10% required rate of return, MRP should trade at 0.9x book value, or about $31.75.

With the Lennar stake sale overhang seemingly solved, I can see shares at 0.9x vs. 0.85x, but I struggle to see a path to book value or a premium to book value. I see shares about ~3% above intrinsic value, and this, to me, is entirely an income stock with limited capital appreciation. With an 8+% yield, that dividend should deliver a market-like return, so there is no need to sell, but investors in MRP should own the stock for income, not appreciation. I continue to see shares as a hold.

data.title: Brunswick: This Cruise Isn't Over
data.description:
Shares of recreational marine engine and propulsion systems company Brunswick Corporation (NYSE:BC) cruised higher on October 23rd after management announced financial results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Although earnings per share fell woefully short of analysts' expectations, both revenue and adjusted earnings per share came in stronger. The company continues to demonstrate that it makes for a great prospect, especially for when we eventually see a recovery in this industry.

Even during tough times, revenue for the company is growing, but I would be lying to you if I said that everything was fine. Profitability still remains problematic. This, combined with how much shares have risen since I reaffirmed the company as a 'buy' back in July of this year, might cause some investors to be cautious about this prospect. But the way I see it, shares are still cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar enterprises. Because of this, I have no problem keeping the company rated a 'buy' for now.

Time to be bullish

October 23rd was a great day to be a shareholder of Brunswick Corporation. The company shot up 8.6% in response to a solid report covering the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. To start with, revenue for the institution came in at $1.36 billion. This represents a nice increase over the $1.27 billion the company reported a year earlier. This rise in sales was broad based, taking place across all four of the firm's operating segments. As an example, the Propulsion segment for the company saw a jump in revenue from $485.9 million to $535.4 million. Management attributed this rise to strong OEM orders that seemed to have been placed by customers in response to their own existing low inventories.

This was not the only area of growth for the company. The Engine Parts and Accessories portion of the business reported a rise in revenue from $336.1 million to $363.7 million. This was primarily led by a 12% surge in its distribution business. Meanwhile, Navico, buoyed by robust demand for marine electronics following the firm's decision to make investments in new offerings and technologies, pushed those sales up marginally from $184.1 million to $186.9 million. And finally, the Boat segment reported a rise in revenue from $345.3 million to $360.2 million. Management chalked this up to the fact that dealer inventories remained 'lean', hinting at the possibility that this rise was thanks to low inventories of its customers leading up to that point.

Overall, revenue for the company ended up being $98.6 million more than what analysts were hoping to see. But that wasn't all. On the bottom line, the company did report a loss per share of $3.57. That was far worse than the $0.71 per share in profits reported the previous year, and it fell short of analysts' expectations by an astounding $4.43. It is important to be mindful, however, that this was only because of $333.8 million, up from the $12.2 million reported the previous year, associated with restructuring, exit, and impairment charges. On an adjusted basis, earnings per share actually fell more modestly from $1.17 to $0.97, taking adjusted net income down from $125.9 million to $106.4 million. But even though that represented a decline year over year, adjusted earnings per share still ended up coming in $0.11 greater than what analysts were hoping to see.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year. Even though revenue and most cash flow and profit metrics for the company worsened during this window of time, management is optimistic about this year in its entirety. They still expect revenue of around $5.2 billion, which would be just a hair below the $5.24 billion reported for last year. They also still expect adjusted earnings per share of about $3.25, which would mean around $213.5 million in adjusted net income. However, free cash flow is now supposed to be in excess of $425 million, which represents an upward revision from the $400 million previously anticipated. And management is now forecasting a total of $80 million of share buybacks.

That's not going to be hard to accomplish considering that, for the first nine months of this year, the company bought back $65 million worth of stock, with that number growing to $70 million subsequent to the end of the quarter. This comes at a time when the company continues to pay out dividends, with the total for the first nine months amounting to $84.6 million. The firm is accomplishing all of this while simultaneously focusing on growth initiatives, such as expanding its Freedom Boat Club to roughly 440 locations globally in the quarter compared to the 433 it had in the second quarter, and also while targeting $200 million of debt reduction this year.

As I mentioned already, for this year, adjusted earnings should be around $213.5 million. And if we project out results based on current guidance and what we have seen already for this year, we should probably anticipate adjusted operating cash flow of around $442.8 million and EBITDA of $563.3 million. With these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are quite attractive multiples if you ask me, especially if we assume an eventual return to the kinds of profitability that the company saw last year. In the table below, I then compared it to five other similar firms. On a price-to-earnings basis, only one of the firms was cheaper than BC is. Two were cheaper on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. And when it came to the price to operating cash flow approach, Brunswick Corporation ended up being the cheapest of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Brunswick Corporation 20.7 10.0 11.2
MarineMax (HZO) 10.6 56.2 12.8
BRP Inc. (DOOO) 26.9 10.1 10.2
Malibu Boats (MBUU) 43.7 11.7 10.3
MasterCraft Boat Holdings (MCFT) 71.2 10.1 12.9
Marine Products Corporation (MPX) 22.6 16.1 16.8

In the last paragraph, I mentioned the prospect of the company eventually returning to the levels of profitability we saw last year. And honestly, that might occur faster than expected. Management has already provided their initial thoughts regarding 2026. They expect price increases, market share gains, and improvements in their discounting strategy, to push revenue up at a mid to high single-digit rate. The company is also expected to have its tariff mitigation strategy fully implemented by that time, which should help considerably.

That's because this year the company expects $75 million of tariff exposure. But with mitigation strategies in place, management expects earnings per share to grow by 25% or more next year. I would be surprised if results are as strong as this. And that's only because I believe that the US economy is heading toward a recession. And during a recession, purchasing boats is something that a large chunk of society will shy away from. I'm not saying that I expect results to worsen. They could, but that's not necessarily a projection. But even if we see another year like this year is shaping up to be, I would consider the outlook to be favorable.

Takeaway

Fundamentally, things are going really well for Brunswick Corporation right now. The company is on solid footing, and the future, from an operational standpoint, looks bright. Shares are cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies. And while I think this industry might face headwinds longer than what management is forecasting, eventually the industry will return to strength. This combination of factors leaves me confident in my previous 'buy' rating for the stock.

data.title: Pony AI's Hong Kong IPO Can Boost Upside Potential
data.description:
Pony AI (NASDAQ:PONY) has registered for an initial public offering (IPO) in Hong Kong, which can bring a further upside catalyst for the stock.

Introduction to Pony AI

Pony AI Inc. is a Chinese autonomous vehicle company co-located in Silicon Valley, Beijing, and Guangzhou. The company was founded by Dr. James Peng and Tiancheng Lou, former Baidu developers. Pony has been embarking on a series of partnerships to advance autonomous driving technology, ride-hailing, and driverless big trucks.

Pony AI's Hong Kong IPO is a positive

Pony AI has completed a filing in the last week with the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) for a planned Hong Kong IPO. The company is aiming to 102,146,500 ordinary shares on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange.

Pony previously went public with a U.S. Nasdaq listing in November 2024, raising $260 million, and later issued a further $153 million in Class A shares to large investors.

Hong Kong's stock exchange has been the global leader in IPOs this year, and Pony will join at an opportune time. The Chinese stock market was massively undervalued, as I noted in 2024. Many large-cap Chinese stocks have soared after domestic and overseas investment returned. Having a domestic investor base for Pony could be a big deal for the company going forward, as it can add increased liquidity from Chinese investors. U.S. investors have often shunned Chinese shares due to issues over transparency, geopolitics, and currency.

Chinese shares have touched a ten-year high in the last two weeks, and there is a big appetite for AI stocks amongst domestic investors.

Pony is moving partnerships at a rapid pace

Pony AI has been very active recently in setting up new partnership opportunities. A Middle East expansion is underway with robotaxi permits in Dubai and Qatar. The company also announced its entry into the Singapore market, with local service provider ComfortDelGro.

A big partnership with U.S. vehicle manufacturer Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) was signed to jointly develop and test SAE Level 4 (hands-off, eyes-off) autonomous vehicles, adopting Stellantis' AV-Ready Platform with Pony's AV technology. This deal also covers battery-electric medium-sized vans, which allows the company to operate in the commercial space as competition heats up for driverless cars.

Jeffries started coverage on Pony AI and their bullish view is driven by the belief that China's robotaxi market is transitioning from pilot to scale, with a total addressable market expected to reach RMB52 billion ($7.4B) by 2030.

Johnson Wan said Pony can be a winner in the market due to regulatory leadership, "cost-down roadmap, and full-stack capabilities across OEMs, TNCs, and system innovation."

"We see it as best positioned to harness network effects and margin expansion as autonomous mobility scales, capturing 30% of the domestic market long term," Wan added.

Jeffries analysts applied a $32.80 price to the shares, which is upside of around 60% from the current level. I agree with this initial target based on the growth seen in Q2 as I will highlight lower.

Early entry to autonomous trucking

Chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA) shared details of a tie-up between Pony AI and Chinese heavy vehicle manufacturer SANY for Level 4 autonomous trucks.

Nvidia has an interest due to the fact that Pony utilizes its DRIVE Orin technology on its autonomous driving controller (ADC).

"The addressable market for trucking is substantial. In 2021 alone, heavy truck sales in China exceeded 1.3 million units, the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers reported," Nvidia noted.

Additionally, "persistent worker shortages are putting pressure on the industry, with drivers making up 25-30% of the total cost of long-haul trucking."

Nvidia shared a humorous description of AV levels, saying Level 4 vehicles were 'Offices and cinemas on wheels,' while Level 5 were 'The Lexus 2054 from Minority Report.'

Recent data showed that there is a trucking "crisis" in the U.S. with many companies filing for bankruptcy and the slate is potentially being cleared for a new lower-cost business model.

I believe Pony has a unique opportunity to address the trucking market when legacy car manufacturers are focused on robotaxis.

Current financials and valuation

There is little information at present on the company's valuation due to the early-stage opportunity. The price/sales metric on a trailing twelve-month basis is currently around 59x. However, on a forward basis it is around 88x, according to Seeking Alpha data. Competitor WeRide (WRD) has a lower valuation at around 40x sales on a trailing twelve-month basis but had lower Q2 revenue at US$17.8 million. WeRide has a lower cash position on its balance sheet at $570.6 million. As a comparison, Tesla (TSLA) has a price/sales of around 15x sales, which would put Pony AI in line with this metric if it saw a threefold increase in revenue. Pony's valuation is indicative of early-stage growth companies, but there is strong upside potential from the addressable market of future AV adoption.

However, the company's progress was seen in the latest Q2 earnings release in August.

CEO James Peng said: “Since mass production started two months ago, over 2,001 Gen-7 Robotaxi vehicles have rolled off the production line, putting us firmly on track to hit the year-end 1,000-vehicle target. Our robust Robotaxi revenues more than doubled, with fare-charging revenues surging by over 300% year-over-year."

"Among all participants in the World Artificial Intelligence Conference (“WAIC”) 2025 in Shanghai, we were the only provider offering fully-driverless and on-demand ride-hailing services to the public," and the "only one to remain fully-operational during extreme weather conditions, including typhoons and heavy rainstorms."

Pony's Q2 revenue for 2025 was up 75.9%, from $12.2 million to $21.5 million. That increase was mainly driven by Licensing and Applications revenues. Cost of revenues was sharply higher with a 47% gain to $18 million from $12.2 million, but gross margin of 16.1% in Q2 was up from a negative 0.3% in the second quarter of 2024. Management cited a "focused strategy on prioritizing high-margin revenue sources" for the improvement.

But the company's cost base is rising with the attempt to scale, and research and development expenses were a big part of the equation at $49 million, up from $29 million in Q2 2024.

R&D costs were drive by: i) investments in mass production for the Gen-7 vehicles and ii) increased employee compensation and benefits to strengthen technological capabilities.

Selling, general, and administrative expenses were $15.7 million, up from $8 million.

Pony AI's latest earnings highlight the early-stage expansion push and it is too soon to focus on the metrics in great detail. Despite that, the company has executed well on its vehicle production and is focused on margin improvement. The company posted a net loss of $53 million for the latest quarter, which is almost identical to the R&D spend. A healthy cash hoard of $747 million is sitting on the balance sheet as of the end of Q2.

Looking more closely at the valuation, I think the Q2 numbers can see the valuation picture improve. After kicking off mass production in June and July, the company has already produced 200 vehicles. They are now on course for a 1,000-vehicle target this year. Based on those figures, it would suggest a 200 per month production rate and a target of around 2,400 vehicles in 2026.

With revenue up 76% year-over-year and registered users up 136% to Q2, the company also saw fare-charging revenues higher by more than 300% year-over-year. Those trends could see revenue double again in 2026, and that would cut the price/sales valuation in half. It also looks to be the start of a long-term production of a growing AV fleet.

Risks to the investment thesis

The talk of a bubble in technology stocks has gone from a few voices to a chorus in the last week or so. If investors get spooked, then tech valuations could come under further pressure. That happened in 2022 with the sharp rise in U.S. interest rates, which dampened demand for speculative investments.

In the autonomous driving space, technology is heating up, but there is also the safety risk. Fellow Chinese firm Xiaomi saw its shares slump almost 9% in one day after a fatal crash. Too many of these events could turn the tide of government and the public adoption of autonomous vehicles.

On the competition front, there is strong competition rising from companies like Waymo, Tesla, and WeRide for autonomous vehicles and robotaxis. I have highlighted the autonomous trucking feature of Pony AI, and I believe that gives the company early entry into a nice area of AV. There is some risk from Tesla's interest in autonomous heavy-duty vehicles. Microsoft founder Bill Gates doubts their feasibility, but Elon Musk says they are the "future of trucking." Although Musk's firms are strong competition, the company may be stretched too thin for manufacturing resources as it targets humanoid robots and robotaxis alongside its current focus of energy storage and vehicles.

The path to autonomous adoption and dominance may not be a straight line, and investors should account for that in position sizing.

Conclusion

Pony AI is setting up partnerships at a rapid pace and has some appealing opportunities, such as the one with Stellantis. The company's move into autonomous trucks is also a great move in my opinion. Early movers into AV technology are focused on cars and taxis, which threatens a crowded market. With the positives for a future stock advance in place, I also believe that many investors are the ignoring the potential for domestic inflows that the Hong Kong IPO can bring. U.S. investors often look for homegrown companies first and the arrival of Pony AI on the Hong Kong market could bring a rush of Chinese investors. The Q2 trends on revenue, fleet, and user base are setting up for a strong 2026.

data.title: KKR Real Estate Finance Trust: Trying To Lend Their Way Out Of The Rut
data.description:
Thesis

The KKR Real Estate Finance Trust (NYSE:KREF) is a CRE REIT we have not covered before, although we did write a piece on their preferred shares last year. With the Fed intent on lowering Fed Funds significantly and the ancillary benefits to the real estate sector, we are going to have a look at KREF's balance sheet, performance, and outlook.

CRE REITs are a particular breed

A REIT is a company that owns income producing real estate, and in the case of CRE REITs, we are talking about commercial real estate. KREF is an entity that is like a sort of 'mini-bank' via its business model: it borrows cheap funds and then lends securely against commercial real estate. In good times the entity makes a spread between the cost of funds and what it clips on the lending, a spread that is passed on to investors. In bad times that spread compresses, and the entity takes hits from bad loans. The hits are taken by the equity slice in the capital structure.

The purpose of this generic introduction is to set the stage for the mindset here - KREF does not produce widgets, and thus cannot come up with a new innovative idea tomorrow that would move its P/E ratio to 60x. The fund is stuck with a portfolio of loans, and the only way out of trouble is to generate more spread via increased lending. The business model here is either go bust, or try to lend your way out of trouble. In the next section we are going to see how KREF is trying to generate spread via lending its way out of a distressed situation.

Latest financials - stable book but no dividend coverage

Now let us take a look at the KREF financials with a very simple mindset as outlined above. Do they make enough cash via their business model to make it sustainable?

As we can see for the quarter ended September 2025, the company has a net interest income of $25 mm at the most basic level, which translates into a net income to shareholders of only $8 mm (we are going to go through the other line items shortly). So KREF has only $8 mm or 0.12 / share of common holders, but it ends up paying 0.25 per share. The math says this is unsustainable since the extra cash comes from borrowings. As we can see from the above table, historically the fund has not been able to cover what it pays for a while, and kindly keep in mind the REIT already cut its dividend once in early 2024. Hence the urgency to lend more in order to generate a higher net income so that payments can be covered. Unless the REIT manages to cover its cash outlays, it will be forced to cut its dividend yet again.

Let us now look closer at the income statement - please note we have highlighted two more line items, namely the REO revenue and REO expenses. The REIT has put a number of buildings into REO ('real estate owned') because the equity holders defaulted on their loans. But now you have to understand that the original equity holders defaulted not because things were grand, but because occupancy issues caused damage to projected cash flows. What we want to point out is that the REO portfolio on the REIT's balance sheet is generating negative net income currently: the REO income is much smaller than the REO expenses. And this math has been in place for quite a number of quarters now. While we are not accountants or experts in the field, we would bet a shiny penny that selling those properties now would result in further write-downs for the REIT.

The one piece of good news in all this kerfuffle? The book value seems to be stabilizing:

We are seeing a stabilization in the low teens here, although we have to say that the market does not believe that book value (and, to be fair, neither do we). The common shares are currently trading at $8.6 per share, which is only roughly 63% of book. This means that the market is doing its own 'mark to market' and is saying that if the fund were to divest all its bad loans and properties now, it would have to take additional loan loss provisions. As we saw from the income statement, the name did not take any significant new provisions in the latest quarter.

As a retail investor, one does not have the ability to go property by property and project cash flows, so it is best to believe the market. If macro conditions improve and valuations improve in the underlying holdings, the fund might see its market value move up towards the book value. However, there are many 'ifs' in this equation.

REO does not mean a problem is solved

Another item to notice is how the fund accounts for 'solving' its troubled loans:

In its earnings presentation, the company showed the above slide. We can see how the 'Raleigh Multifamily' property loan, which was on the watchlist now was 'Transferred to REO'. Be cognizant that this transfer on the balance sheet does not solve any problems. It is simply an accounting transfer. As we saw from the income statement, the REO portfolio is currently generating significant operating losses (i.e., the fund would be better off if it had no REO). We are also suspicious regarding valuations for the REO portfolio in today's distressed market. We also see the addition of a new problem loan, namely the 'Cambridge Life Sciences' building loan.

When will the portfolio stabilize?

While institutional investors have access to sophisticated tools like Trepp (a cash flow and analytics platform for CRE and CMBS), retail investors do not, so they have to keep it simple. Just look at the holdings in the fund:

When you look in the 'Investment Date' column and you do not see anything originated before 2022, you will know the fund is back to normality. Rates went up in 2022, and anything of a prior vintage will have unfavorable LTVs.

The fund does present a table of its risk ratings, but they are not very helpful in predicting the future:

As an investor in a CRE REIT, you want loans originated after 2022, and LTVs of 70% or less. Again, this is a very simplistic approach, but one that works for a retail investor without access to cash-flow modelling tools. As of now, the KREF portfolio could see plenty of trouble ahead still.

Our take on KREF - lending its way out of trouble

KREF is a CRE REIT with significant portfolio troubles. The fund has flexibility in valuing illiquid assets, but the market is looking beyond that - the rough 40% discount to book is there because the market does not believe the balance sheet. The fund comes from a very well known fund family in KKR, so it is highly unlikely to default. The remaining choice is very simple - amend, extend, and lend. The entity needs to continue lending at attractive terms in order to generate a positive net income to pay preferred shareholders and common shareholders. Concurrently it is taking buildings in REO, assuming that at some point down the line the market will mend itself and it will be able to sell those buildings at cost or higher:

We continue to see upside in our REO portfolio, where we are making progress. And as we stabilize and sell those assets, we can repatriate that capital and reinvest into higher earning assets. Therefore, there's embedded earnings power of $0.13 per share per quarter that we will be able to unlock over time.

The above quote is from the earnings call transcript, which you can find here, and is corporate talk for waiting for a better market. You need to understand that KREF does not really have a choice here. The only logical path is to try to hold on to assets until the market is better, and in the meantime lend more in order to generate positive net income:

If you look at the balance sheet, you can see the liabilities increasing since December 2024 by much more than the asset side. The REIT is just borrowing more. This is possible because of the KKR name, and the fact that the fund has access to bank facilities and liquidity. If the market moves higher, this will be a smart move. If there is an actual recession and the market (CRE market) moves lower, the increased leverage might not be the best idea.

The takeaway here is very simple: KREF is a high beta name that is a bet on the mending of the CRE market. As a retail investor, we would not invest in the common shares here, but choose names with much better balance sheets like Blackstone Mortgage Trust, Inc. (BXMT) and Starwood (STWD). If one has a strong view on the CRE market and wants to bet on a recovery, KREF will very likely move back toward its book value in such a scenario, thus a massive +40% upside. But again, this is more of a bet, and we would think such an investor would be very familiar with CRE cash-flow modelling and assumptions.

Conclusion

KREF has posted results for its most recent quarter, and the news is a mixed bag. While the book value is stabilizing, the fund is continuing to see a distressed portfolio with yet another property put into REO. The REO portfolio is generating a negative net income figure, thus detracting from the performance. The current distribution on the common shares is not fully covered; thus, the fund is trying to lend more in order to generate a higher net spread. We are on 'Hold' with respect to the common shares, which we consider a high-beta play on the CRE market, and would much rather look at players with better balance sheets like BXMT and STWD.

data.title: Tesla: On A Mission To Accelerate The World's Transition
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Rowan Street Q3 2025 Letter.

In 2025, we made just one new investment — Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) — during a period of widespread pessimism. Back in March and April, sentiment around the company and its CEO was near rock bottom. Headlines were dominated by controversy, and investors were fleeing what they perceived as chaos. We saw an opportunity.

This is precisely what we do at Rowan Street. Our sweet spot is patiently waiting for moments of dislocation — and stepping in with conviction while others hesitate. It’s how we built many of our most successful positions: Meta (META) in 2018 and again in 2022 when sentiment was bleak… Shopify (SHOP) during the 2022 tech collapse… Spotify (SPOT) and Netflix (NFLX), which we were buying in early 2022 precisely when others — including some of the most respected investors in the world — were throwing in the towel. We applied the same philosophy with Adyen (OTCPK:ADYEY) in 2023, after the market overreacted to short-term margin pressure, and again with Dino Polska (OTCPK:DNOPY) in 2024, when the stock sold off sharply following a temporary slowdown in revenue growth and margin compression, driven by deflationary food prices and higher marketing expenses.

Each of these investments came from the same discipline — waiting patiently for quality to get mispriced, then acting boldly when it does. This is the essence of how we operate: conviction through dislocation. Tesla is simply the latest expression of that philosophy.

We initiated our position at roughly $235 per share, investing about 8% of our capital at cost. Since then, the stock has appreciated roughly 75%, and it now represents around 12% of our portfolio — quickly becoming the third-largest position in the fund. While we never anchor our conviction to short-term price moves, that early performance serves as a reminder that markets occasionally offer brief windows of mispricing — and that our job is simply to act with conviction when they do.

We’ve followed Tesla for years. In fact, we first studied it deeply back in 2020 after reading Ashlee Vance’s biography of Elon Musk. I remember being mesmerized by Musk — a rare combination of vision, determination, extreme focus, and relentless work ethic that sets him apart even among the greats like Jobs, Bezos, Gates, and Zuckerberg. His vision often feels as if it came straight out of a science fiction novel — and yet, he always finds ways to make it real.

Back then, Tesla was still a car company — capital-intensive, vulnerable to macro shocks, and far from the multi-layered moats it has since built. Our admiration for Musk’s brilliance wasn’t yet matched by conviction in the business’s long-term durability. That patience cost us a tenfold gain. Mr. Market can be a tough teacher — and he charges full tuition, upfront. But it bought us something more valuable: perspective. It taught us that timing and conviction must align — and that a true understanding of a business’s moat takes years to mature.

Fast forward to today: Tesla has evolved into one of the most formidable and strategically advantaged companies in the world — combining manufacturing excellence, data dominance, and real-world AI leadership in a way no other company has achieved. What once looked like a car company now resembles a vertically integrated technology and energy platform with multiple self-reinforcing moats.

Tesla’s Multi-Layered Moat

1. Manufacturing Scale & Integration — The Foundation

Tesla’s manufacturing advantage is the foundation of everything else. The factory itself is the product — a masterpiece of vertical integration and engineering efficiency. From gigacasting and structural battery packs to “Dojo-in-the-loop” manufacturing optimization, Tesla builds what no one else can replicate at scale.

Most competitors are still in the “production hell” Tesla survived years ago. The result is a cost structure and speed of innovation that traditional automakers can’t match. This isn’t just a car company — it’s a manufacturing flywheel compounding know-how and efficiency every quarter.

2. The Data Flywheel (FSD & AI) — Tesla’s Brain

With over six million vehicles on the road feeding billions of miles of real-world driving data into Tesla’s neural networks, the company has built the largest autonomous-driving dataset on Earth — a compounding advantage that grows stronger every day. Each mile driven improves Tesla’s Full Self-Driving system, which makes the cars safer and more capable, attracting more drivers, generating more data — the essence of a flywheel.

Tesla’s proprietary AI stack, powered by Dojo, processes an ocean of video data to train neural networks that handle the unpredictable situations human drivers face daily. Unlike competitors relying on synthetic data or geofenced testing, Tesla learns in the real world, from the real world.

To understand the economics, consider utilization. A typical car is used only 10 hours per week out of 168 — roughly 6% of the time. Once fully autonomous, that same vehicle could operate 50 to 60 hours a week, delivering passengers by day and cargo by night. As Musk put it: “A car that used to sit idle 90% of the time can now work for you while you sleep. That increase in utilization could represent the largest asset value increase in human history.”

Tesla’s advantage isn’t just data — it’s approach. Competitors like Waymo rely on expensive sensor suites and low-volume production, which makes their systems economically unscalable. Musk noted: “Waymo’s cars cost four or five times more to make and are produced in tiny quantities. Tesla’s cost 20–25% as much and are made in the millions.”

That’s why Tesla’s AI improves exponentially faster. Every car on the road is a data-gathering node that makes the system smarter.

Tesla has even solved the hardest edge cases — driving toward direct sunlight, in fog, or at night — using a “direct photon count” approach that bypasses traditional image processing. As Musk explained, “We can see in what appears to be the blackest of night, and through fog, probably better than the average person.”

This combination of scale, cost efficiency, and technical mastery gives Tesla a moat few companies in history can match.

3. Distribution & Ecosystem Lock-In — The Network

Tesla’s direct-to-consumer model keeps its brand and customer relationships unmediated by dealers. Its global Supercharger network — now becoming the standard even for competitors — adds another layer of lock-in. Every Tesla on the road is a connected device receiving over-the-air software updates, FSD upgrades, and app-based integrations. This creates network effects and switching costs that compound over time.

4. Brand & Aspiration — The Emotion

Tesla isn’t just an automaker; it’s a cultural symbol of progress and innovation. Like Apple (AAPL), it evokes status and emotional connection — customers don’t just buy a Tesla, they join a movement. That kind of brand power is rare and deeply durable.

5. Platform Optionality — The Future

Tesla’s final layer of advantage lies in its expanding platform. Full Self-Driving and the future robotaxi network could transform every Tesla from a depreciating asset into an income-producing one. Energy storage and Megapacks extend the business into the global power grid, while Optimus — Tesla’s humanoid robot — pushes its AI and manufacturing capabilities into entirely new industries. Musk has stated that ~80% of Tesla’s value will be Optimus humanoid robots, and that he believes Optimus will be the biggest product of all time by far.

Crucially, Tesla’s decision to operate its own robotaxi network — instead of partnering with Uber (UBER) — further strengthens that moat. It’s classic Musk: vertically integrate everything that matters. Uber’s business model depends on paying human drivers, who capture roughly 75% of every fare, while Tesla’s robotaxis eliminate that cost entirely. Without drivers, Tesla’s cost per mile collapses, allowing it to offer cheaper rides while earning superior margins.

For context, Uber today has about six million active drivers and couriers globally — translating to only a few million cars on the road at any given time, most of which sit idle for roughly 90% of the week. Once autonomous, Tesla’s vehicles could operate five to ten times more hours per week — say, 50 to 60 hours instead of 10 — effectively turning 400,000 robotaxis into the capacity of two million traditional vehicles. Combine that with zero driver costs and no 25–30% platform fee to Uber, and the economics tilt decisively in Tesla’s favor.

By controlling the full stack — manufacturing, software, and network — Tesla keeps the economics, pricing power, and customer relationship entirely in-house. That vertical integration gives Musk freedom to experiment with ultra-low pricing, even to the point of making car ownership unnecessary. His goal isn’t to participate in Uber’s model — it’s to make it obsolete.

If Tesla executes on this vision, it could become one of the most valuable companies in history — perhaps, as Musk said, “worth more than the next five companies combined.” That may sound audacious, but so did reusable rockets, mass-market EVs, and landing boosters on drone ships — until he made them real.

Most great companies have one primary moat. Tesla is building at least five, all reinforcing one another. That’s why it could become one of the widest and most durable competitive moats of our era — more akin to a modern-day Standard Oil or Apple than to a traditional automaker.

In Tesla, we see the rare combination of visionary leadership, structural advantage, and compounding potential that defines the investments that built Rowan Street.

Reallocation from Spotify to Tesla

In the first half of 2025, we continued trimming our Spotify position and reallocated the proceeds into Tesla. Spotify remains a strong founder-led compounder and continues to execute well. However, at current valuations, much of Spotify’s expected growth and margin expansion already appear well reflected in the stock. While we continue to hold a core position in Spotify, we view its competitive advantages as relatively stable rather than expanding meaningfully — especially when compared to the scale and optionality of Tesla’s opportunity set.

Tesla, by contrast, stands out as one of the most exceptional businesses of our time — led by the most extraordinary entrepreneur of our lifetime, whose vision and relentless execution are driving breakthroughs across autonomy, AI, energy, and robotics. We established the majority of our position back in March and added further in mid-August as our conviction continued to deepen.

We’ve owned Spotify for more than seven years and have developed a deep understanding of its business, its moat, and its management. It remains a very good company — but Tesla is a truly great one. In our view, Elon Musk is building one of the widest and most consequential moats in business history, and that’s exactly the kind of opportunity that deserves to sit at the center of our portfolio.

DISCLOSURES

The information contained in this letter is provided for informational purposes only, is not complete, and does not contain certain material information about our fund, including important disclosures relating to the risks, fees, expenses, liquidity restrictions and other terms of investing, and is subject to change without notice. The information contained herein does not take into account the particular investment objective or financial or other circumstances of any individual investor. An investment in our fund is suitable only for qualified investors that fully understand the risks of such an investment. An investor should review thoroughly with his or her adviser the funds definitive private placement memorandum before making an investment determination. Rowan Street is not acting as an investment adviser or otherwise making any recommendation as to an investor’s decision to invest in our funds. This document does not constitute an offer of investment advisory services by Rowan Street, nor an offering of limited partnership interests our fund; any such offering will be made solely pursuant to the fund’s private placement memorandum. An investment in our fund will be subject to a variety of risks (which are described in the fund’s definitive private placement memorandum), and there can be no assurance that the fund’s investment objective will be met or that the fund will achieve results comparable to those described in this letter, or that the fund will make any profit or will be able to avoid incurring losses. As with any investment vehicle, past performance cannot ensure any level of future results. IF applicable, fund performance information gives effect to any investments made by the fund in certain public offerings, participation in which may be restricted with respect to certain investors. As a result, performance for the specified periods with respect to any such restricted investors may differ materially from the performance of the fund. All performance information for the fund is stated net of all fees and expenses, reinvestment of interest and dividends and include allocation for incentive interest and have not been audited (except for certain year end numbers). The methodology used to determine the Top 5 holdings is the largest portfolio positions by weight. The top 5 do not reflect all fund positions. The Top 5 can and will vary at any given point and there is no guarantee the fund will meet any specific level of performance. Net returns presented are net of fund expenses and pro-forma performance fees. Rowan Street Capital does not charge fixed management fees.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
